sentences.text: Mainly from villages.
We were concentrating on railroads.
Our partisans were mining railroads, getting everything they could from the trains.
They were rail-- derailed, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] involved in a lot-- a lot of battles-- battles.
And the battle started that the Germans knew of our existing.
And even so, we moved nights and slept days.
They knew about us.
And they-- there was a lot of shooting.
There was a lot of shooting and warning.
We had scouts.
We were very good.
We were like little animals to survive.
So we were much, much faster and smarter than they were.
We had to.
So we had our scouts posted many miles, so we had a lot of time to prepare ourselves to run.
We advised the scout to make a lot of commotion that we could hear the shooting.
The minute we heard shooting, we were on our feet and running the opposite way, of course.
We were very close with Germans.
Luckily for us, that we had more people.
We had a whole battalion.
We were a lot of people.
And they used to come with a car or two.
Now, how did they expect to fight partisans?
And they didn't have enough soldiers.
OK.
The soldiers were busy in-- in Russia to get Russia for themselves.
So they were successful because of the fear of the Germans that one soldier, a German soldier, could take over a whole village.
That's how fearful the people were.
And that's how they succeeded.
They succeeded that way, but not with partisans.
OK?
When there were two, three cars, we didn't leave one alive.
Survival.
It's them or us.
So the ones that were guiding the railroads, it didn't work, because we could say kill them with no-- no problems, no problems at all.
So there are a lot of problems we gave them with transportation-- a lot-- from Russia to Poland and so on.
So that Ukrainian part, we took care.
All right?
And they could not really succeed, but we lost a lot of people, a lot of people, like any other war.
And we tried to get new ones-- new members to join us.
So that was--
And the day was usually sleeping, the nights working.
And when there was shortage of food, we had to send people to get-- that was their mission, to get some pigs and kill them and have them with us, whatever it is.
And bread and whatever we had to take, even forcibly, because we felt that we are protecting these people and they had to help us.
They didn't want to give us these pigs.
They didn't want to give us the chickens.
They didn't want to give us the bread.
OK.
I don't say that we erased it completely and gutted out from them.
Most of the time, the orders were to leave them, that they should be able to eat and survive.
So we kind of shared.
All right.
But then some of them were wise guys, did not want to give us.
And it was hard.
It was hard.
Only that I did not-- did not approve when they caught these people that were against partisans and were given out secrets.
They were cruel.
They were trying to get the information, and after they got the information, they killed him.
So I didn't approve it, or maybe I was too young to see such cruelty.
But then when we came into villages that were killed and burned by the Germans, then you'd think twice.
Survival.
What they did to the people, how they tortured some of the people, unbelievable.
You would never believe it.
So much blood was of innocent people around.
Yeah.
They welcomed us.
They-- they actually welcomed us with open arms.
But that took time, persuading and going through the-- the work.
So life there, one outfit.
You change once a week.
Food.
If you eat once a day, you were lucky.
And sometimes you had to go through with bread and water for-- for a whole day.
But then some of the times you really had a picnic.
You got pork.
You got meat.
You got other things.
And we had very talented cooks that we're preparing and saving every scrunch of bread.
Myself?
No.
And thank God, because I was in the-- in the medical part.
Thank God, because otherwise I would be a very bad killer.
Maybe, you know something, if I would see it's my life against the other one, live or die.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Killing and killing.
And when I left partisans, I had dreams and nightmares through years.
Tortures and everything else, how they caught partisans get the-- to get the information, what they did to it.
I would not go into details.
Helped out.
Definitely.
They were very cooperative.
They cooperated with the Germans.
No.
The only thing, we had one incident is that two partisans had a duel, because of their political beliefs.
And they shot each other.
One survived, the other one was killed.
And that survived was wounded very badly and went through torture.
That's the only thing inside the partisan.
Otherwise, one was killing for the other one, like a family.
It was very quiet and friendly.
Does not matter.
At that time, it's survival.
It's helping.
It's your cause.
You were ready to get-- your got your speeches, and you were ready to kill for-- for your cause, for your countrymen.
[LAUGHS] No names.
We called them Daiadia.
D-A-I-A-D-I-A.
In Russian, it's uncle.
You can call him anybody, you know.
Daiadia.
Russian.
Russian.
He was Russian.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- they made sure that they gave me-- after the war, we were in a village.
We got all our honors and-- and medallions, whatever you want to do.
And then we went to Ostruck.
Ukraine.
Ukraine only.
And mainly, I would say, close to Shepetovka.
OK?
The railroad-- Shepetovka had a very sophisticated railroad.
So we-- maybe not sophisticated, but a very busy German railroad.
So we were concentrating on that.
But we were moving around that I couldn't remember, so many villages.
Woods.
When I say villages, I mean woods near the villages.
Woods-- 85% woods.
As partisans?
Very hard.
Very hard.
That was our home.
You-- villages you can see, you can get there one, two, three.
But try to get us in-- in the woods, we are like little animals.
So it was very hard for the Germans to fight us.
And they didn't want to lose that many people.
They really did not want to lose, because every fight they had with the partisans-- I know about ourselves-- 80-- no, 90% we were the winners.
Enough.
We had enough ammunition.
How they got it, it's my recollection is only when they robbed the train, the trains that were derailed.
Oh, very often.
And including my boyfriend that I told you that was burned alive.
That was his specialty.
And we had-- and that was our specialty.
There were partisans that fought in the back of the front.
OK?
In the back on the, you know, German side.
OK.
So they were preparing like for Russians to have it easier catching the towns and things like that.
There were partisans like that.
But ours, its mission is mischief for Germans.
But in the mischief, do you think there were people at the top that were military-- military, actually knew exactly what they were doing in setting this up?
Very professional.
Very-- we couldn't survive.
Very professional.
I don't say all of them, but the top people, very professional.
And I suspect that they were top in their Russian-- they were sent to form this unit.
No way.
No way.
It was all secret.
It was all coded.
It-- it was just like an army.
No.
That's-- that's-- friend.
[INAUDIBLE] and that's it.
OK.
Mainly the first name, initial.
OK.
The initial, but when-- after the war, then we of course some of us that need it, did give their names.
The Russians.
Me personally, we were near [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that-- Shepetovka but further, you know, further to Poland.
Yeah.
Deeper in Poland.
Shepetovka was that.
Just the only stories, not stories, but happening that that many people were killed, and they never passed our path that we could have saved them.
They never passed our path, because if they would, they would be saved.
But stories we heard.
And I didn't have to have the stories.
We came to villages, and some of them were burned to the ground.
People hanging from feet and hands.
Horrible things happened during the war.
Ukrainian and Polish.
Oh, yeah.
There were no Jews anyplace.
No.
You wouldn't-- you would be probably surprised if I tell you that some of them were anti-Semitic.
They were.
The would say, oh, if that would be a Jew, he would do this and this and this.
And calling like somebody would be a lower class, like one partisan wouldn't do the right thing or be clumsy or something, they would say what are you, a [INAUDIBLE]?
Like a Jewish name.
Well, you can't do it.
What are you, a [INAUDIBLE]?
But not all of them.
The highest ranks, no.
No.
And they were against it.
As a matter of fact, we had a meeting, OK, that I mentioned.
I didn't say Jewish, but I mentioned, I said, it's not very nice to point out that this race or this religion or this religion is different, as we are here brothers and sisters.
And I don't think that we want to insult each other.
I was Ukrainian.
They were Russian.
They asked me to go into details.
I said no, I don't know the details exactly.
I mean, I know the details that curses, I said, that they were cursing each other.
And that involved Russian, Ukrainian, and other nationalities.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
We had meetings very often.
You could state your griefs.
You could point out what's wrong.
Russian people are very liberated people, you know, that they speak up.
They speak up.
They're-- they're not afraid.
That's how they were brought up.
You have something to say, speak up.
There's a meeting and speak up.
Yeah.
If you would ask me, I would say that is Russian-- [INAUDIBLE] Russian partisans, OK, because there were Polish partisans, there were Ukrainian partisans.
But ours, I would say majority Russian.
And was the leaders two quarters from Russia.
So I'm confident that that's what it is.
Oh, they knew of each other.
They-- they-- they had to know, because we could not concentrate all of us in one place.
We knew that partisans like that, they are five kilometers.
They knew that 20 kilometers this operates.
There constantly were people that worked with it, even so we didn't have the telephones.
Oh, yeah.
When one Partisan came to a place, partisan group, we right away we sent messenger to the other ones, that be careful.
We are here.
No.
Absolutely not.
Every one of them cooperated, because the Polish would not go to the Russians.
So they usually favored the Polish partisans.
So there were groups.
They did their job, and we never duplicated a mission.
If we took over the railroad, that is our job and it's our railroad, and our district.
This is where we operate.
And the other one, because otherwise it would be a disaster.
When I say medals, it's little red, like pieces of material with stars and a little something.
Unfortunately, I lost it someplace.
But anyway, I came home, and I found out that my mother was dead.
I didn't know about it that she died.
She died, and I-- we suspect on a heart attack, that she had a heart attack.
Then I found out that all of our children-- when I say all, five of us were survivors.
It was joyful.
It was beautiful.
We tried to help each other.
We took over in Ostruck our older sister's house that she lived before the war.
The one that was killed, Mancia, and our house was completely destroyed by the war.
And we all lived in two rooms there.
And after a while, seeing that we really cannot survive together, we started to go places.
My young brother joined Polish Army.
My sister was with my older sister, [?
Jean. ?] Manya [?
Jean. ?] I took off to Rowne-- Rowne.
And I got a nice position in [INAUDIBLE], which is a very high judge-- judges and lawyers place.
And I could go get there that position, it's through [INAUDIBLE], a woman.
Again, a woman that lived in our study.
I came.
I saw her.
She accepted me like her daughter.
As a matter of fact, she treated me like her daughter.
She advised me, she did things.
To be young and, you know, pretty during the war, it wasn't easy.
She was like my mother.
She was protecting me and doing things for me.
I had a very good job.
They gave me a room, and I was situated very nicely there.
After that, I met a young gentleman that was a partisan too.
And we decided that we want to get out of all of it.
And we moved to Lwow.
I took my younger sister with me, and we decided to marry.
Then from Lwow, in Lwow I got married.
I decided that my sister lived in-- in Russia-- in Ukraine.
I left her in Ukraine.
And my brother was in the army.
I had-- I could move further without having them.
I took my older sister in.
When she came in to Lwow, then we, two of us, went to the Polish Army and over there I had a few dollars paid off to call my brother.
So I had all the family, and we moved to Byten.
In Byten, I had a house with my husband.
My first husband.
Mischa ?] Steinberg.
I called him Manny.
And he supported all of us.
We had two German ladies that maked us very comfortable.
He himself was a businessman, taking big trucks with cigarettes from our area, from Byten and to the different countries.
So he was making a lot of the money and make us very comfortable.
Then there was the end.
We had to think of future.
Byten was not our future.
My husband decided that he wants to go to America.
And he worked on it and had enough money to pay for other things.
And we did travel to America.
Before going to America from Byten, we had to cross the border to Germany.
And there were Polish militia that watched very carefully, but we were connected with a Jewish organization that got all the Jews through the borders.
So they were specialists.
So when you had to take Jews out of certain country and send them to Israel, this is what they did.
So we got these specialists, and they took us as Greeks.
We're not supposed to speak any language.
We're not supposed to understand anything.
As we were there, and they saw military places, because they caught us crossing, and that's supposed to be the thing that's supposed to be happening.
And the Polish people were concentrating on me and my sister, and they didn't know that we understand Polish.
And they were setting up how could they rape both of us.
[LAUGHS] But thank God that we did understand.
We acted properly, and they did not succeed, of course.
And we took a train on the German border, and we went to Munich.
Outskirts of Munich had the DP camps, displaced person camps.
And that was called Mozach.
I wish I knew.
I'll try my best.
M-O-Z-A-C-H.
Mozach.
That was outskirts of Munich.
There we were till my sister and my brother, being that they are young, could go to Israel.
The gate was open for them.
They could go without the planning and not to wait too long.
All the young children could have gone to a certain age.
Now, are-- were stuck, because we were too old.
So we sent out my sister and my brother to Israel.
They went in kibbutz, and we went-- my sister and myself and my husband-- we went to United States.
We started from the Neu Freimann.
It's the Mozach.
Neu Freimann and Mozach.
Actually, Neu Freimann is the camp.
Neu Freimann.
Freimann.
It is-- no.
It's a secret one.
No.
I don't know.
It's a secret.
I could not-- I didn't know even.
I didn't know the names of the people.
So it was secretly.
In United States in '47, October 27, 1947.
And HIAS was our leading people, and they gave us an apartment, by the way, without heat.
And gave us jobs in factory, brassiere and girdle factories.
Both of us went and worked.
My husband went to work as a butcher.
And we made a living.
And up till that point, HIAS was supplying us with clothes and food and the apartment.
They left us when we were good enough to support ourselves.
Oh, after that we found a better apartment.
My sister was-- got married, and her husband was selling vacuum cleaners.
Jean. ?] And she moved out.
I and my husband were trying to build a life.
My husband was concentrating on buying a butcher store, because since he was working in a butcher line.
I tried to get in the office.
OK.
